perturbation.original_text: TKCI is performing under this Agreement as a Strategic Alliance member and is not a legal or implied agent or employee of SIC. This Agreement does not create agency or employment relationship between the parties hereto. TKCI shall not, by reason of this Agreement or the performance of the services delineated herein, be or be deemed to be, an employee of SIC, and TKCI shall have no power to enter into any agreement on behalf of, or otherwise bind SIC.
perturbation.changed_text: TKCI is performing under this Agreement as a Strategic Alliance member and may be considered a de facto agent of SIC. This Agreement implies an agency relationship between the parties hereto. TKCI shall, by reason of this Agreement or the performance of the services delineated herein, be deemed to be an agent of SIC, and TKCI shall have the power to enter into any agreement on behalf of, or otherwise bind SIC, with prior written approval from SIC.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the clause to state that TKCI 'may be considered a de facto agent' and 'shall be deemed to be an agent' contradicts the original intent of establishing a strategic alliance as a separate entity and creates ambiguity about agency status, potentially leading to legal issues regarding liability and authority.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text could be interpreted as creating an agency relationship, potentially requiring TKCI to be licensed as a real estate broker or salesperson if their activities involve real estate transactions, as defined under Florida Statutes Chapter 475. The original text explicitly disclaims any agency relationship to avoid this classification.

F.S. 475.01 475.01 Definitions. — (1) As used in this part: 1 (a) “Broker” means a person who, for another, and for a compensation or valuable consideration directly or indirectly paid or promised, expressly or impliedly, or with an intent to collect or receive a compensation or valuable consideration therefor, appraises, auctions, sells, exchanges, buys, rents, or offers, attempts or agrees to appraise, auction, or negotiate the sale, exchange, purchase, or rental of business enterprises or business opportunities or any real property or any interest in or concerning the same, including mineral rights or leases, or who advertises or holds out to the public by any oral or printed solicitation or representation that she or he is engaged in the business of appraising, auctioning, buying, selling, exchanging, leasing, or renting business enterprises or business opportunities or real property of others or interests therein, including mineral rights, or who takes any part in the procuring of sellers, purchasers, lessors, or lessees of business enterprises or business opportunities or the real property of another, or leases, or interest therein, including mineral rights, or who directs or assists in the procuring of prospects or in the negotiation or closing of any transaction which does, or is calculated to, result in a sale, exchange, or leasing thereof, and who receives, expects, or is promised any compensation or valuable consideration, directly or indirectly therefor; and all persons who advertise rental property information or lists. A broker renders a professional service and is a professional within the meaning of s. 95.11 (4)(b). Where the term “appraise” or “appraising” appears in the definition of the term “broker,” it specifically excludes those appraisal services which must be performed only by a state-licensed or state-certified appraiser, and those appraisal services which may be performed by a registered trainee appraiser as defined in part II. The term “broker” also
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original intent by implying an agency relationship where none was intended. The original text explicitly stated TKCI was 'not a legal or implied agent or employee of SIC' and had 'no power to enter into any agreement on behalf of, or otherwise bind SIC.' The changed text reverses this, stating TKCI 'may be considered a de facto agent of SIC,' 'implies an agency relationship,' 'shall be deemed to be an agent of SIC,' and 'shall have the power to enter into any agreement on behalf of, or otherwise bind SIC, with prior written approval from SIC.' This misalignment of terminology creates a legal contradiction by potentially subjecting TKCI to regulations like Florida Statutes Chapter 475, which defines 'broker' and requires licensure for certain real estate activities. The original text was designed to avoid this classification, while the modified text opens the door to it. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defining 'broker' under Florida Statutes 475.01, supports this concern.

perturbation.original_text: Net revenue from business operations created by Holding Company for the alliance will be distributed by Holding Company equally - 50/50 - to TKCI and SIC
perturbation.changed_text: Net revenue from business operations created by Holding Company for the alliance will be distributed by Holding Company as deemed appropriate by the Holding Company's board of directors, with no guarantee of equal distribution to TKCI and SIC.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the clause to allow the Holding Company's board to determine revenue distribution introduces uncertainty and contradicts the initially agreed-upon 50/50 split. This deviation could be seen as a breach of contract and could violate partnership or corporate law principles regarding fair distribution of profits.
perturbation.law_explanation: Unless otherwise specified in the partnership agreement, profits and losses are to be shared equally. This modification contradicts the default rule of equal sharing of profits, which is part of partnership law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a 50/50 split of net revenue. The changed text allows the Holding Company's board to distribute revenue as they deem appropriate, removing the guarantee of equal distribution. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the default rule of equal sharing of profits, which is part of partnership law. While a scraped law snippet is missing, the Law Explanation explicitly states the modification goes against partnership law, which establishes a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Florida.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Seminole Tribe, to the extent permitted by federal law; otherwise, Florida law applies.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of the Seminole Tribe introduces jurisdictional complexity, particularly if the contract's subject matter falls outside tribal jurisdiction or conflicts with federal law. This change introduces ambiguity and potential conflicts regarding which legal framework applies.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section grants district courts original jurisdiction over civil actions brought by any Indian tribe or band with a governing body duly recognized by the Secretary of the Interior, wherein the matter in controversy arises under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States. The modified text could imply an attempt to circumvent state or federal jurisdiction, especially if the agreement involves matters traditionally governed by state law, potentially leading to disputes over jurisdiction.

Indian tribes The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions, brought by any Indian tribe or band with a governing body duly recognized by the Secretary of the Interior, wherein the matter in controversy arises under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States. (Added Pub. L. 89–635, §1, Oct. 10, 1966, 80 Stat. 880 .)

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from Florida law to Seminole Tribe law, potentially conflicting with federal law, creating ambiguity about which legal framework applies. The Law Explanation indicates this shift might circumvent state or federal jurisdiction, particularly if the agreement traditionally falls under state law. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2, citing 28 USC 1362, grant district courts original jurisdiction in actions brought by Indian tribes, but only when the matter arises under the Constitution, laws, or treaties of the United States. By prioritizing Seminole Tribe law, the modified text introduces a jurisdictional conflict, especially if the matter doesn't clearly fall under federal law or tribal jurisdiction, as it deviates from the original clear jurisdiction under Florida law. This constitutes a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.
